## Required content

### Theorem: Milne Theorem 3.2 — rational-map extension to an abelian variety
Statement (Milne *Abelian Varieties* §I.3, Theorem 3.2): Let `X` be a smooth variety over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, and let `f : X ⇢ A` be a rational map into an abelian variety `A`. Then `f` extends uniquely to a regular morphism `f̃ : X → A`.

### Proof sketch (Milne §I.3 verbatim, then project notation)

Quote the Milne proof verbatim. Then split into sub-steps:

1. **Codim-1 reduction**. Reduce to showing `f` has no codim-1 indeterminacy. Use Lemma 3.3 (A.4.a) to extend over codim-2 points once codim-1 is excluded.
2. **Exclusion of codim-1 indeterminacy via Auslander–Buchsbaum**. Suppose for contradiction `f` has a codim-1 indeterminacy point `x`. By A.4.b (Auslander–Buchsbaum on a smooth variety), the local ring `O_{X,x}` has depth ≥ 1, hence `f` extends across codim-1 points by Hartshorne III.8 / standard valuation argument.
3. **Combine**. Codim-1 + codim-2 extensions glue to give a global regular morphism `X → A`.

53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules

161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.

183	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

61	## Caveats
62	- Open-access lecture notes (definitive author's copy on Milne's site), PDF-only — no
63	  LaTeX source published, so exact statements must be quoted from the PDF.
64	- Default standing hypothesis in §III.6: `C` is a complete nonsingular curve of
65	  **genus g > 0** with a k-rational point `P`. The Albanese universal property
66	  (Prop 6.1/6.4) is stated under those hypotheses.
67	- Conventions (§"Conventions concerning algebraic geometry", p.v): "variety" =
68	  geometrically reduced separated scheme of finite type, nonclosed points omitted.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface $S$ over a field --- such as $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$
28	or the product $C \times C$ of a smooth projective curve $C$ with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension $2$. To extend a rational map
30	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from $S$ to an abelian variety across a codimension-$2$ closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} $2$, hence a rational function with no codimension-$1$ poles on a neighbourhood of $x$
33	extends across $x$. The depth $\geq 2$ inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring $R$ one has $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$,
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension $d$ admits an $R$-regular sequence of length $d$ generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$, i.e.\ $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its $\text{Ext}$ reformulation
47	    (Definition~\ref{def:depth} and Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~\ref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~\ref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.

61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	  [Depth of a module]
66	  \label{def:depth}
67	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
68	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
69	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
70	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
71	  % "Let $R$ be a ring, and $I \subset R$ an ideal. Let $M$ be a finite $R$-module.
72	  %  The {\it $I$-depth} of $M$, denoted $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is defined as follows:
73	  %  \begin{enumerate}
74	  %  \item if $IM \not = M$, then $\text{depth}_I(M)$ is the supremum in
75	  %  $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences in $I$,
76	  %  \item if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$.
77	  %  \end{enumerate}
78	  %  If $(R, \mathfrak m)$ is local we call $\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)$ simply
79	  %  the {\it depth} of $M$."
80	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
81	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring, let $I \subseteq R$ be an ideal, and let $M$ be a finitely
82	  generated $R$-module. The \emph{$I$-depth} of $M$, written $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is the
83	  supremum in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences contained
84	  in $I$, provided $IM \neq M$; if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$. When
85	  $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
86	  $\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)$ and call this simply the
87	  \emph{depth} of $M$. The depth of the ring $R$ itself is $\text{depth}(R)$, the depth
88	  of $R$ as a module over itself.
89	\end{definition}
90	
91	Recall that a sequence $f_1, \dots, f_r \in R$ is called \emph{$M$-regular} if
92	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0$ and for each $i$, the element $f_i$ is a non-zero-divisor on
93	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M$. The empty sequence is regular on $M$ iff $M \neq 0$ (it is
94	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention $\text{depth}(0) = \infty$
95	above is the usual one).
96	
97	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
98	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the $\text{Ext}$ characterisation (used to establish an
99	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	  [Depth via $\text{Ext}$]
103	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
104	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
105	  \uses{def:depth}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
107	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
108	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
109	  % "Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$.
110	  %  Let $M$ be a nonzero finite $R$-module. Then $\text{depth}(M)$
111	  %  is equal to the smallest integer $i$ such that
112	  %  $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$ is nonzero."
113	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
114	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
115	  $\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}$ and let $M$ be a nonzero finitely generated $R$-module. Then
116	  \[
117	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
118	  \]
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
122	% "Let $\delta(M)$ denote the depth of $M$ and let $i(M)$ denote
123	%  the smallest integer $i$ such that $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$
124	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that $i(M) < \infty$.
125	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-ideal-nonzerodivisor} we have
126	%  $\delta(M) = 0$ if and only if $i(M) = 0$, because
127	%  $\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)$ exactly means
128	%  that $i(M) = 0$. Hence if $\delta(M)$ or $i(M)$ is $> 0$, then we may
129	%  choose $x \in \mathfrak m$ such that (a) $x$ is a nonzerodivisor
130	%  on $M$, and (b) $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1$.
131	%  Consider the long exact sequence
132	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
133	%  $0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}:
134	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
135	%  Since $x \in \mathfrak m$ all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
136	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
137	%  Lemma \ref{lemma-annihilate-ext}.
138	%  Thus it is clear that $i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1$. Induction on
139	%  $\delta(M)$ finishes the proof."
140	\begin{proof}
141	  \uses{def:depth}
142	  Write $\delta := \text{depth}(M)$ and $i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}$.
143	  The base case $\delta = 0$ is exactly $\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0$ (some element of
144	  $M$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$, i.e.\ $\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)$, i.e.\
145	  $\mathfrak{m}$ contains a zero-divisor on $M$), which is $i = 0$. For the inductive step
146	  pick $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ a non-zero-divisor on $M$ with
147	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1$. The short exact sequence
148	  $0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0$ induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
149	  -)$. Multiplication by $x$ annihilates each $\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
150	  $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ and $\kappa$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$), so the long exact
151	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
152	  $\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ at the relevant
153	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by $1$. By induction
154	  $i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1$, so $i = \delta$.
155	\end{proof}

157	\section{Projective dimension}
158	\label{sec:ab_pd}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	  [Projective dimension]
162	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
163	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
164	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
165	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
166	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
167	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
168	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
169	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
170	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring and let $M$ be an $R$-module. The \emph{projective
171	  dimension} of $M$, written $\text{pd}_R(M)$, is the infimum in
172	  $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths $n$ of projective resolutions
173	  \[
174	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
175	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
176	  \]
177	  with each $P_i$ a projective $R$-module. We say $M$ has \emph{finite projective dimension}
178	  if $\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty$, and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
179	  convention $\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty$ (some references); we use $\text{pd}_R(0) = 0$ here
180	  (the empty resolution $0 \to 0$ of length $0$), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
181	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} concerns only nonzero $M$.
182	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated $R$-module of finite projective
183	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each $P_i$
184	  free of finite rank and each transition map $P_{i+1} \to P_i$ landing in $\mathfrak{m} P_i$.
185	\end{definition}
186	
187	The two basic facts about $\text{pd}_R(M)$ needed downstream are:
188	\begin{itemize}
189	  \item $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ iff $M$ is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
190	    iff $M$ is free);
191	  \item if $0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0$ is a short exact sequence with $P$ projective, then
192	    $\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1$ provided $K \neq 0$.
193	\end{itemize}
194	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
195	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.

198	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}

200	\begin{lemma}
201	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
202	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
203	  \lean{RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact}
204	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
205	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
206	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Let $R$ be a local Noetherian ring. Let $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$
209	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite $R$-modules.
210	  %  \begin{enumerate}
211	  %  \item
212	  %  $\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}$
213	  %  \item
214	  %  $\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}$
215	  %  \item
216	  %  $\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}$
217	  %  \end{enumerate}"
218	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
219	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring and let
220	  $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$ be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
221	  $R$-modules. Then
222	  \begin{enumerate}
223	    \item $\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)$;
224	    \item $\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)$;
225	    \item $\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)$.
226	  \end{enumerate}
227	\end{lemma}
228	
229	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
230	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
231	%  $\Ext^i(\kappa, N)$, see Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-ext},
232	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
233	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
234	%  from Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}."
235	\begin{proof}
236	  \uses{lem:depth_via_ext}
237	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}, $\text{depth}(N')$ (resp.\ $\text{depth}(N)$,
238	  $\text{depth}(N'')$) is the smallest index at which $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$ is
239	  nonzero on $N'$ (resp.\ $N$, $N''$). Apply the long exact $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$
240	  sequence to $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$:
241	  \[
242	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
243	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
244	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
245	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
246	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
247	  \]
248	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
249	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
250	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
251	  the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}.
252	\end{proof}

259	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}

360	  yields the chain of inequalities

362	  $\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2$, \dots,

369	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both $R$ and $M$. Indeed: either $\text{pd}_R(M) > 0$, in
370	  which case $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
371	  of a minimal resolution, or $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ in which case $M$ is finite free and
372	  $\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0$. Either way both $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ and
373	  $\text{depth}(M) > 0$, hence $\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0$ by
374	  Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
375	  \mathfrak{m}$ exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of $M$ as above; the snake
376	  lemma applied to multiplication by $x$ gives a minimal finite free resolution
377	  \[
378	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
379	  \]
380	  of $M/xM$ over $R/xR$, of the same length $e$. So
381	  $\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)$. By Stacks
382	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both $\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$ and
383	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1$, and one verifies
384	  $\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$ (regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}$
385	  correspond to regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}/(x)$). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
386	  $M/xM$ over $R/xR$:
387	  \[
388	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
389	  \]
390	  Substituting yields
391	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$, i.e.\
392	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)$, as required.
393	\end{proof}

395	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
396	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
397	
398	\begin{definition}
399	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
400	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
401	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
402	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
403	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
404	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
405	  % "A Noetherian local ring $R$ is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
406	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
407	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
408	  A Noetherian local ring $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
409	  $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$ (equivalently, if $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
410	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite $R$-module $M$ means
411	  $\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)$, cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
412	\end{definition}
413	
414	\begin{corollary}
415	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
416	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
417	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
418	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local, thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
419	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
420	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
421	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
422	  % "Let $R$ be a regular local ring and let
423	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ be a minimal set of generators
424	  %  for the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$. Then
425	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ is a regular sequence, and
426	  %  each $R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)$ is a regular local ring
427	  %  of dimension $d - c$. In particular $R$ is Cohen-Macaulay."
428	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
429	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is
430	  regular local of Krull dimension $d$, then $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$.
431	\end{corollary}
432	
433	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
434	% "Note that $R/x_1R$ is a Noetherian local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$
435	%  by Lemma \ref{lemma-one-equation} with $x_2, \ldots, x_d$
436	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
437	%  Since $R$ is a domain by Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-domain}
438	%  $x_1$ is a nonzerodivisor."
439	\begin{proof}
440	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local}
441	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be regular of dimension $d$ and pick a minimal generating set
442	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ of $\mathfrak{m}$. By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), $R$ is
443	  an integral domain, so $x_1$ is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient $R/x_1 R$ is a Noetherian
444	  local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$ (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
445	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by $x_2, \dots, x_d$, hence
446	  has dimension exactly $d - 1$ and is again regular of dimension $d - 1$. Inducting on $d$,
447	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ is an $R$-regular sequence, so
448	  $\text{depth}(R) \geq d$. The reverse inequality $\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d$
449	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
450	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$, i.e.\
451	  $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay.
452	
453	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
454	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take $M := R$ itself. Then $M$ is
455	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank $1$), so $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$. The
456	  formula gives $\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)$, a tautology. The non-tautological
457	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension $d$, every
458	  finitely generated module $M$ has $\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d$ (Stacks tag 00O2,
459	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an $M$ then
460	  relates $\text{depth}(M)$ to $d - \text{pd}_R(M)$, which is the form most often used in
461	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
462	  $R$ Cohen--Macaulay itself.
463	\end{proof}

465	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
466	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
467	
468	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
469	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from a regular projective surface $S$ to an abelian variety $A$
470	across a closed point $x \in S$ that is missing from the domain of definition $U \subseteq S$.
471	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
472	
473	\begin{enumerate}
474	  \item The local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
475	    dimension $2$ (because $S$ is regular and $\dim S = 2$).
476	  \item By Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
477	    $\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2$. Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
478	    $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$-regular sequence of length $2$ (any two transverse generators).
479	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_S$ on $U = S \setminus \{x\}$ extend
480	    uniquely across $x$ because $H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ and
481	    $H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
482	    $\geq 2$ at $x$ by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
483	  \item For a rational map $f : S \dashrightarrow A$ with codimension-$\geq 2$ indeterminacy
484	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-$\geq 2$ input produces a sheaf-theoretic
485	    extension of $f$ to all of $S$: pull back any sheaf $\mathcal{O}_A$-affine-locally,
486	    obtain sections over $U$, and extend across $x$ by the above.
487	\end{enumerate}
488	
489	So Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
490	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
491	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
492	Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} are formalized in
493	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
494	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.

\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
% codim-$\geq 2$ extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
% ``regular local $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay''
% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
% rational map $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ to a regular morphism $J \to A$,
% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$
% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
%
% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
%
% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
% creating it.

\section{Setup and statement}
\label{sec:thm32_setup}

Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, a smooth variety $X$ over $\bar k$ (in the
project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
finite type over $\bar k$ that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
and an abelian variety $A$ over $\bar k$ (in particular $A$ is complete, smooth, and a
group object in $\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k$). A rational map
$f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to A$ defined on a dense open subset
$U \subseteq X$, two such pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$ and $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ being equivalent
if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$.

The result of this chapter is:

\begin{theorem}
[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map $\alpha\colon V--\to A$ from a nonsingular
  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of $V$."
  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}

  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, let $A$ be
  an abelian variety over $\bar k$, and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ be a rational
  map. Then $f$ is defined on the whole of $X$: there is a unique regular morphism
  $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$ whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
  set of definition $U \subseteq X$ of $f$ agrees with $\varphi_U$.
\end{theorem}

The uniqueness clause is immediate from $X$ being reduced and separated: two regular
morphisms $X \to A$ that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-$\geq 2$
extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
structure result for rational maps to a group variety:

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
  pure-codimension-$1$ structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.

  Concretely: let $U \subseteq X$ be a maximal open set of definition of $f$, and let
  $Z := X \setminus U$ be its complement.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
      to codim $\geq 2$), applied with the normal variety $X$ and the complete variety
      $A$, we have $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-$1$
      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety $A$), the closed subset
      $Z$ is either empty or of pure codimension $1$ in $X$.
    \item Both conditions together force $Z = \emptyset$: a non-empty pure-codimension-$1$
      closed subset has codimension exactly $1$, contradicting $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Therefore $U = X$ and $f$ admits a regular representative on all of $X$, which is the
  required regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$. Uniqueness is the standard
  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-$1$ step
  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
  $\mathcal O_{X, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ --- works because $X$ is normal
  and $A$ is complete) and a codim-$\geq 2$ step (depth-$\geq 2$ at codim-$\geq 2$ points,
  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
  depth-$\geq 2$ input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
  for a regular local ring $(\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)$ of Krull dimension $d$, the
  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to $M = R$
  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d$, so in particular
  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2$ whenever $\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2$.
  Concretely, when $X$ is a smooth surface (e.g.\ $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ or
  $C \times C$ for a smooth projective curve $C$), every closed point $x$ is regular of
  dimension $2$, so $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is Cohen--Macaulay of depth $2$; sections of
  $\mathcal O_X$ on $U = X \setminus \{x\}$ extend uniquely across $x$, and the same is
  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of $\mathcal O_A$ along $f$.

  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-$\geq 2$ half. The dependency
  $\uses{thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}$ is recorded on
  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
\end{remark}

\section{Why the proof is char-free}
\label{sec:thm32_char_free}

Each of the two inputs is char-free:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-$\geq 2$ extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-$\geq 2$ extension
    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
    $\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A$ and
    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
    on $A$ (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of $X$ (so divisors and
    DVRs at codim-$1$ points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
    has a characteristic restriction.
\end{itemize}
Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)

\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}

The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$. Milne's proof of
Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ factoring a given pointed regular morphism
$\varphi \colon C \to A$ through the canonical $f_P \colon C \to J$ as follows. The
symmetric power $C^{(g)}$ admits a regular morphism $\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A$
defined by
\[
  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
\]
and a birational morphism $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J$ (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
seeks a morphism $J \to A$ making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ followed by descent to $J$; the rational map
$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because $C^{(g)}$ is
a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and $A$ is an abelian variety. Without
\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
shortcut ($J \cong J^\vee$) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
\cref{chap:Jacobian}.

\section{Lean encoding}
\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}

The Lean target file is the new
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
this chapter targets is the theorem
\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
notation):
\[
  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over $\bar k$]} \;\to\;
  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety $\bar k$} \;\to\;
  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
\]
The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
in the three-step pattern above (codim $\geq 2$ from A.4.a + pure-codim $1$ from A.4.a
$\Rightarrow$ empty indeterminacy locus).

The Mathlib namespace target is
\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.

No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.

\section{Out of scope}
\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}

This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
